schools.summary: Paul anticipates the perverse inference from 5:20 (grace abounding where sin abounds) — shall we continue in sin that grace may abound? — the antinomian cavil twisting grace into a license for sin (the slander of 3:8), raised to be refuted (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the turn from justification to sanctification — grace, far from licensing sin, is the very power that destroys it; the objection raised to be utterly refuted.

themes: shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound
the antinomian objection
grace twisted into license
the turn to sanctification

schools.summary: Paul vehemently rejects the cavil ('God forbid,' me genoito) — for the Christian is dead to sin (by union with Christ's death, sin's dominion broken, Barnes, Calvin), and how shall one dead to sin live any longer in it? The absurdity is plain.
Calvin (with Theodoret): the contradiction — it is absurd for one dead to sin to live in it; the new life is incompatible with continuing in sin.

themes: God forbid
how shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein
dead to sin
the impossibility of continuing in sin

schools.summary: Baptism signifies union with Christ, incorporation into him ('baptized unto Christ,' the public proclamation of an inward spiritual relation that precedes the sign — Paul was not a sacramentarian, Robertson), and particularly into his death, fellowship with his death (Barnes, Ellicott).
Cyril (with Calvin): baptism is not a bare sign but the counterpart of Christ's sufferings — by it we are made partakers of his death, the old man put to death, not merely a grace of remission.

themes: baptized into Jesus Christ
baptized into his death
union with Christ in baptism
fellowship with Christ's death

schools.summary: We are buried with Christ by baptism into death (the allusion to immersion, the body going under as a burial, picturing death to sin and Christ's burial, Barnes, Clarke, Wesley), that, as Christ was raised by the Father's glory, we should walk in newness of life; Barnes argues the scope is to teach death to sin, not to prescribe the mode (the obligation from the fact of baptism, not the mode).

Calvin (with Theodoret): baptism includes what it symbolizes when rightly received with faith — the death of Christ efficacious to destroy our flesh, his resurrection to renew us; the believer raised with Christ must walk in newness of life.

debates.question: Does 'buried by baptism' prescribe immersion as the required mode?

sides.position: It alludes to immersion's symbolism, but the scope is to teach death to sin — the obligation arises from the fact of baptism, not the mode
It plainly alludes to the ancient manner of baptizing by immersion

themes: buried with him by baptism into death
walk in newness of life
baptism as burial and resurrection
the immersion symbolism and death to sin

schools.summary: The believer is planted together (symphutoi, grown together, as a graft to a stock, an intimate organic union, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) with Christ in the likeness of his death (dying to sin) and shall be also in the likeness of his resurrection (rising to new life), the one guaranteeing the other.

Calvin (with Chrysostom): not mere imitation but a real ingrafting and secret union — Christ's virtue transferred to us, his death destroying our corruption, his resurrection renewing us; we pass from our own nature to his.

themes: planted together in the likeness of his death
we shall be also in the likeness of his resurrection
the organic union with Christ
ingrafting, not mere imitation

schools.summary: 'Our old man' is the corrupt, sinful nature inherited from Adam (the whole depraved self before renewal, Ephesians 4:22), crucified with Christ — put to death (a painful subduing of sin, Barnes; pictured in baptism not effected by it, Robertson) — that 'the body of sin' (the mass of corruption) might be destroyed, that we should no longer serve sin.

Calvin (with Theodoret): the old man is the whole nature we bring from the womb, slain by the power of Christ's cross; the body of sin is the corrupted mass, rendered inoperative — not that we cease entirely to sin, but that we become at last victorious in the contest.

themes: our old man is crucified with him
that the body of sin might be destroyed
the crucifixion of the old man
the breaking of sin's dominion

schools.summary: 'He that is dead' (has died with Christ) is freed (released, acquitted) from sin — death dissolving sin's claim and dominion, as death ends a master's claim on a slave (Barnes, Robertson); the believer dead with Christ set free from sin's bondage (Calvin, Theodoret).

themes: he that is dead is freed from sin
death frees from sin
release from sin's dominion
the dead beyond the master's claim

schools.summary: If we be dead with Christ (to sin, the foundation laid, vv.2-7), we believe we shall also live with him — both the present new life and the future eternal life, sharing his risen life (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): union with Christ's death guarantees union with his life — the believer who has died with him shall surely live with him.

themes: if we be dead with Christ
we shall also live with him
life with Christ following death with him
union in death and life

schools.summary: Christ, once raised, dies no more (his resurrection life endless, unlike Lazarus who would die again, Barnes, Robertson); death has no more dominion over him — he has conquered it forever.
Calvin (with Theodoret): as Christ's risen life is permanent and beyond death's dominion, so the believer's new life, united to him, is secure and abiding.

themes: Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more
death hath no more dominion over him
Christ's endless risen life
death's dominion broken

schools.summary: Christ died unto sin once for all (ephapax, a single, unrepeatable death, bearing sin once to put it away, Barnes, Robertson) and now lives unto God (his risen life wholly devoted to God, in communion and glory).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the pattern — as Christ died once to sin and now lives unto God, so the believer, united to him, is to be dead to sin once for all and to live unto God.

themes: he died unto sin once
he liveth unto God
Christ's once-for-all death and life
the pattern for the believer

schools.summary: The believer is to reckon (logizesthe, count, regard as a settled fact, by faith) himself dead to sin and alive to God through Christ — applying Christ's pattern (v.10) to himself; the reckoning a deliberate act of faith laying hold on the reality (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the first command of the chapter — to know and reckon the truth of our union with Christ; not pretense but faith laying hold on the reality of being dead to sin and alive to God.

themes: reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin
alive unto God through Jesus Christ
the great application of the reckoning of faith
dead to sin, alive to God

schools.summary: Since the believer is dead to sin (vv.2-11), he must not let sin reign (hold kingly dominion) in his mortal body — the seat of sin's assault through its appetites — nor obey its lusts (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): sin remains and tempts but must not be obeyed — the believer is to refuse its dominion, not yielding to the lusts of the body.

themes: let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body
that ye should obey it in the lusts thereof
the exhortation against sin's reign
active resistance to sin's dominion

schools.summary: The believer is not to yield his members (bodily faculties) as instruments (hopla, weapons) of unrighteousness unto sin, but to yield himself wholly unto God, as one alive from the dead, and his members as instruments of righteousness (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the decisive consecration — the whole self and all its faculties surrendered to God's service, the positive counterpart to refusing sin.

themes: yield not your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin
yield yourselves unto God
the exhortation to consecration
members as instruments of righteousness

schools.summary: Sin shall not have dominion, for the believer is not under the law (the covenant of works/legalism, which exposes sin but gives no power) but under grace (the dispensation of mercy that pardons and gives power by the Spirit to conquer sin, Barnes, Calvin, Wesley); Theodoret notes grace adds help where the law only commanded.

Gill: not a mere exhortation but an absolute promise — sin shall not lord it over the believer; not its damning power only (already removed) but its governing, tyrannical power broken, sin dethroned and Christ reigning.

themes: sin shall not have dominion over you
ye are not under the law, but under grace
the promise of victory over sin
law exposes, grace empowers

schools.summary: Paul anticipates the misuse of v.14 — if under grace and not law, may we sin freely? (the antinomian abuse in a new form) — and vehemently rejects it ('God forbid,' me genoito); being under grace is no license to sin but the opposite (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): grace, far from licensing sin, makes us servants of righteousness (vv.16-18) — the answer to the cavil.

themes: shall we sin, because we are not under the law, but under grace
God forbid
the renewed antinomian objection
grace is no license to sin

schools.summary: Whomever one yields to and obeys, that one is his master — obedience determines servitude (Barnes, Robertson); there are only two services, sin unto death or obedience unto righteousness, no neutral ground.
Calvin (with Henry): the one under grace who obeys God is God's servant, not sin's — so grace is no license to sin; obedience determines mastery.

themes: to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are
whether of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness
the principle of servitude
one is the servant of whom he obeys

schools.summary: The believers were once servants of sin (their former state, now past), but have obeyed from the heart (sincerely, inwardly, marking true conversion) that form of doctrine (tupos, the mold or pattern of the gospel) into which they were delivered and shaped; the thanksgiving to God who wrought the change (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: ye were the servants of sin, but ye have obeyed from the heart
that form of doctrine which was delivered you
thanksgiving for the changed service
heart-obedience to the gospel mold

schools.summary: The believers, made free from sin (its bondage and dominion), became servants of righteousness — not freedom into autonomy but a change of masters; Christian freedom is a glad servitude to righteousness, freed from one master to serve a better (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the paradox — true freedom is the service of righteousness; the believer exchanges the tyranny of sin for the blessed servitude of God.

themes: being then made free from sin
ye became the servants of righteousness
the result of the changed service
freedom as the service of righteousness

schools.summary: Paul apologizes for the slave-metaphor (speaking 'after the manner of men' because of their weakness, to make the truth vivid), drawing the contrast — as they once yielded their members to uncleanness and iniquity, so now they are to yield them to righteousness unto holiness, the same wholeness of devotion now to a holy end (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the goal — the service of righteousness issues in holiness (sanctification), the progressive growth in holiness as its fruit.

themes: I speak after the manner of men because of the infirmity of your flesh
yield your members servants to righteousness unto holiness
the exhortation from the slave-figure
full devotion to righteousness unto holiness

schools.summary: When they were slaves of sin, they were 'free' from righteousness — a bitter, ironic freedom, untouched and uncontrolled by righteousness (Barnes, Robertson); no true liberty but the worst bondage, wholly given to sin.
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul recalls their former state to set off the shamefulness of sin's service and the blessedness of the new.

themes: when ye were the servants of sin, ye were free from righteousness
the former state
the bitter freedom of sin's slavery
free from righteousness

schools.summary: Paul asks what fruit (profit) they had from their former sins — the answer being none worth having, only what they are now ashamed of (Barnes, Robertson); the enlightened conscience blushing at the former sins, shame the only present harvest, and the end death.
Calvin (with Henry): the terminus — the final issue of those sins is death (eternal ruin); sin's fruit shameful now, deadly at the last.

themes: what fruit had ye then in those things whereof ye are now ashamed
the end of those things is death
the worthless fruit of sin
sin's shameful, deadly harvest

schools.summary: Now freed from sin and made servants to God (the happy reversal of the old state), the believers have fruit unto holiness (growth in sanctification) and the end everlasting life — over against the death that ends sin's service (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Cyril): the terminus — the final issue of God's service is everlasting life, the blessed harvest of holiness, contrasted with the death of sin's service.

themes: being made free from sin, and become servants to God
fruit unto holiness, and the end everlasting life
the blessed fruit of the new service
holiness now, everlasting life at the end

schools.summary: 'Wages' (opsonia, the soldier's pay) means death is the proper, deserved, just pay sin earns (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson); but the deliberate antithesis substitutes 'gift' (charisma) — eternal life is not earned but freely given (Ellicott), and through Jesus Christ our Lord.

Clarke (with Gill): a man merits hell but cannot merit heaven — the apostle says not 'the wages of righteousness is life' but the gift of God; even this gift comes through Christ, procured by his blood and given freely.

themes: the wages of sin is death
the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
the great contrast of wages and gift
death deserved, life freely given
